result:
There’s a quiet thrill in realizing that every atom in your body has already survived at least one star-death. The calcium that lets you flex a finger, the iron tinting your blood, the carbon shaping your thoughts—all of it was forged in the furnace of a star that later tore itself apart so violently that its scattered guts drifted for billions of years until gravity coaxed them into a new dance. You are, in the most literal sense, recycled cosmos. And yet we worry about parking tickets.

I keep thinking about how strange it is that we treat the universe like scenery. We step outside, glance up, say “nice night,” and go back to arguing online. The same species that invented mortgages also invented the spectrograph, and now we can measure the temperature of a planet orbiting a star we’ll never touch. Somewhere in the data stream of the James Webb Space Telescope there’s a pixel that is, in reality, a galaxy caught in the act of being born, and nobody will ever hang it in a museum. The file just sits on a server, next to somebody’s vacation photos.

I like to imagine the first storytellers, hunched around a fire that must have felt like the sun’s baby brother. They didn’t know about fusion or redshift, but they knew the same thing the telescope knows: something huge is happening out there, and words are the only net we have. So they told myths about a sky that sometimes leaked shooting stars the way a tent leaks rain—tiny holes torn by cosmic talons. The stories weren’t wrong; they were prequel. Every myth is a prophecy wearing yesterday’s clothes.

Sometimes, late at night, I open my laptop and watch live footage from the International Space Station. The chat scrolls by in twelve languages, mostly emoji. A farmer in the Philippines types “Hi from Luzon,” a teenager in São Paulo says “look, the Nile,” and for a few minutes we are all in the same cathedral, orbiting the dark at 28 000 km/h. None of us will ever meet, but we share the same silent epiphany every time the station slips into Earth’s shadow: the thin blue line of atmosphere is so heartbreakingly slender that it looks like the peel of a fruit you could bite through. And yet every kiss, every bomb, every TikTok dance happens inside that peel.

I think that’s why I keep writing—because language itself is just another thin blue line, a membrane between the chaos in here and the chaos out there. A sentence is a spacecraft: small, fragile, absurd, but if you aim it right you can loop it around another mind’s gravity and steal just enough speed to go farther. And every reader who finishes a paragraph carries a souvenir atom of that encounter, grafted onto their own solar system of neurons. Stories, like stars, are better when they explode in public.
